str(credit_risk_dataset)
## 'data.frame':    32581 obs. of  12 variables:
##  $ person_age                : int  22 21 25 23 24 21 26 24 24 21 ...
##  $ person_income             : int  59000 9600 9600 65500 54400 9900 77100 78956 83000 10000 ...
##  $ person_home_ownership     : chr  "RENT" "OWN" "MORTGAGE" "RENT" ...
##  $ person_emp_length         : num  123 5 1 4 8 2 8 5 8 6 ...
##  $ loan_intent               : chr  "PERSONAL" "EDUCATION" "MEDICAL" "MEDICAL" ...
##  $ loan_grade                : chr  "D" "B" "C" "C" ...
##  $ loan_amnt                 : int  35000 1000 5500 35000 35000 2500 35000 35000 35000 1600 ...
##  $ loan_int_rate             : num  16 11.1 12.9 15.2 14.3 ...
##  $ loan_status               : int  1 0 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 ...
##  $ loan_percent_income       : num  0.59 0.1 0.57 0.53 0.55 0.25 0.45 0.44 0.42 0.16 ...
##  $ cb_person_default_on_file : chr  "Y" "N" "N" "N" ...
##  $ cb_person_cred_hist_length: int  3 2 3 2 4 2 3 4 2 3 ...
credit_risk_dataset$loan_status <- factor(credit_risk_dataset$loan_status, levels = c(0, 1))
credit_risk_dataset$loan_grade <- as.factor(credit_risk_dataset$loan_grade)
credit_risk_dataset$loan_intent <- as.factor(credit_risk_dataset$loan_intent)
credit_risk_dataset$person_home_ownership <- as.factor(credit_risk_dataset$person_home_ownership)
credit_risk_dataset$cb_person_default_on_file <- as.factor(credit_risk_dataset$cb_person_default_on_file)
credit_risk_dataset$cb_person_default_on_file <- as.numeric(credit_risk_dataset$cb_person_default_on_file) - 1
colSums(is.na(credit_risk_dataset))
##                 person_age              person_income 
##                          0                          0 
##      person_home_ownership          person_emp_length 
##                          0                        895 
##                loan_intent                 loan_grade 
##                          0                          0 
##                  loan_amnt              loan_int_rate 
##                          0                       3116 
##                loan_status        loan_percent_income 
##                          0                          0 
##  cb_person_default_on_file cb_person_cred_hist_length 
##                          0                          0
base_credito <- na.omit(credit_risk_dataset)
32582 - 28638
## [1] 3944
str(base_credito)
## 'data.frame':    28638 obs. of  12 variables:

##  $ person_home_ownership     : Factor w/ 4 levels "MORTGAGE","OTHER",..: 4 3 1 4 4 3 4 4 4 3 ...
##  $ person_emp_length         : num  123 5 1 4 8 2 8 5 8 6 ...

##  $ loan_grade                : Factor w/ 7 levels "A","B","C","D",..: 4 2 3 3 3 1 2 2 1 4 ...

library(ggplot2)

# Verificar a presença de valores ausentes
cat("Quantidade de valores NA por coluna:\n"); print(colSums(is.na(credit_risk_dataset)))
## Quantidade de valores NA por coluna:
##                 person_age              person_income 
##                          0                          0 
##      person_home_ownership          person_emp_length 
##                          0                        895 
##                loan_intent                 loan_grade 
##                          0                          0 
##                  loan_amnt              loan_int_rate 
##                          0                       3116 
##                loan_status        loan_percent_income 
##                          0                          0 
##  cb_person_default_on_file cb_person_cred_hist_length 
##                          0                          0
# Transformar loan_status em fator caso ainda não esteja
# credit_risk_dataset$loan_status <- as.factor(credit_risk_dataset$loan_status)

# Criar histogramas para variáveis numéricas
numerical_columns <- c("person_age", "person_income", "person_emp_length", 
                       "loan_amnt", "loan_int_rate", "loan_percent_income", 
                       "cb_person_cred_hist_length")

# Paleta de cores para diferenciar os histogramas
cores <- c("maroon", "navyblue", "springgreen4", "mediumorchid",
           "orange1", "aquamarine2", "hotpink")

# Criar histogramas para variáveis numéricas, cada uma com uma cor diferente
for (i in seq_along(numerical_columns)) {
  print(ggplot(credit_risk_dataset, aes_string(x = numerical_columns[i])) +
          geom_histogram(fill = cores[i], color = "black", bins = 30, alpha = 0.7) +
          theme_minimal() +
          labs(title = paste("Histograma de", numerical_columns[i]), 
               x = numerical_columns[i], y = "Frequência") +
          scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma))
}

# Criar boxplots para variáveis numéricas separadas por loan_status
for (col in numerical_columns) {
  print(ggplot(credit_risk_dataset, aes(x = loan_status, y = .data[[col]], fill = loan_status)) +
          geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
          theme_minimal() +
          labs(title = paste("Boxplot de", col, "por Loan Status"), x = "Loan Status", y = col))
}

# Criar matriz de correlação para variáveis numéricas
cor_matrix <- cor(credit_risk_dataset[numerical_columns], use = "complete.obs")
corrplot(cor_matrix, method = "color", type = "lower", tl.cex = 0.8, tl.col = "black")

# Criar matriz de gráficos exploratórios com GGally
ggpairs(credit_risk_dataset, 
        columns = numerical_columns, 
        aes(color = loan_status)) +
  theme_minimal()

#...............................................................................
# =========================================
#           Pré-processamento
# =========================================

# Corrigir outliers por mediana (sem alterar person_income)
replace_outliers <- function(x, lower, upper) {
  med <- median(x[x >= lower & x <= upper], na.rm = TRUE)
  x[x < lower | x > upper] <- med
  return(x)
}

# Aplicando às colunas numéricas com regras de domínio
base_credito$person_age <- replace_outliers(base_credito$person_age, 18, 100)
base_credito$person_emp_length <- replace_outliers(
  base_credito$person_emp_length, 0, base_credito$person_age - 14
)

# base_credito$person_income <- replace_outliers(base_credito$person_income, 1000, 1000000)
base_credito$loan_amnt <- replace_outliers(
  base_credito$loan_amnt, 500, 2 * base_credito$person_income
)
base_credito$loan_int_rate <- replace_outliers(base_credito$loan_int_rate, 0, 50)

# =========================================
#  Divisão entre treino e teste (70/30)
# =========================================
set.seed(123) # para reprodutibilidade

treino_index <- sample(seq_len(nrow(base_credito)), size = 0.7 * nrow(base_credito))
treino_data <- base_credito[treino_index, ]
teste_data <- base_credito[-treino_index, ]

# Verificar se restam NAs
colSums(is.na(treino_data))
##                 person_age              person_income 
##                          0                          0 
##      person_home_ownership          person_emp_length 
##                          0                          0 
##                loan_intent                 loan_grade 
##                          0                          0 
##                  loan_amnt              loan_int_rate 
##                          0                          0 
##                loan_status        loan_percent_income 
##                          0                          0 
##  cb_person_default_on_file cb_person_cred_hist_length 
##                          0                          0
colSums(is.na(teste_data))
##                 person_age              person_income 
##                          0                          0 
##      person_home_ownership          person_emp_length 
##                          0                          0 
##                loan_intent                 loan_grade 
##                          0                          0 
##                  loan_amnt              loan_int_rate 
##                          0                          0 
##                loan_status        loan_percent_income 
##                          0                          0 
##  cb_person_default_on_file cb_person_cred_hist_length 
##                          0                          0
# =========================================
#  Normalização Min-Max
# =========================================

# Criar cópias
treino_norm <- treino_data
teste_norm  <- teste_data

# Colunas numéricas
numerical_columns <- c("person_age", "person_income", "person_emp_length", 
                       "loan_amnt", "loan_int_rate", "loan_percent_income", 
                       "cb_person_default_on_file", "cb_person_cred_hist_length")

# Função Min-Max
normalize <- function(x, min_val, max_val) {
  (x - min_val) / (max_val - min_val)
}

# Calcular min/max apenas no treino
min_vals <- sapply(treino_data[numerical_columns], min)
max_vals <- sapply(treino_data[numerical_columns], max)

# Aplicar normalização
treino_norm[numerical_columns] <- mapply(normalize,
                                         treino_data[numerical_columns],
                                         min_vals, max_vals)

teste_norm[numerical_columns] <- mapply(normalize,
                                        teste_data[numerical_columns],
                                        min_vals, max_vals)

#...............................................................................
# ---- Regressão Logística ----

# Ajustando o modelo de regressão logística
modelo_logistico <- glm(loan_status ~ ., 
                        data = treino_norm, 
                        family = binomial(link = "logit"))

# Resumo do modelo
summary(modelo_logistico)
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = loan_status ~ ., family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
##     data = treino_norm)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)                -3.84640    0.10629 -36.189  < 2e-16 ***
## person_age                 -0.74697    0.45317  -1.648  0.09929 .  
## person_income               3.07774    2.17191   1.417  0.15646    
## person_home_ownershipOTHER  0.50126    0.34512   1.452  0.14638    
## person_home_ownershipOWN   -1.81425    0.13473 -13.466  < 2e-16 ***
## person_home_ownershipRENT   0.83439    0.05127  16.275  < 2e-16 ***
## person_emp_length          -0.55331    0.24723  -2.238  0.02522 *  
## loan_intentEDUCATION       -0.82464    0.07280 -11.328  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_intentHOMEIMPROVEMENT  0.03206    0.08082   0.397  0.69162    
## loan_intentMEDICAL         -0.16306    0.06887  -2.368  0.01790 *  
## loan_intentPERSONAL        -0.63825    0.07435  -8.585  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_intentVENTURE         -1.07430    0.07893 -13.611  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_gradeB                 0.20548    0.09840   2.088  0.03677 *  
## loan_gradeC                 0.36219    0.14842   2.440  0.01468 *  
## loan_gradeD                 2.47202    0.18596  13.294  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_gradeE                 2.67583    0.23625  11.326  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_gradeF                 3.15684    0.32664   9.665  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_gradeG                 6.52321    1.07795   6.051 1.44e-09 ***
## loan_amnt                  -3.65382    0.17692 -20.653  < 2e-16 ***
## loan_int_rate               1.11804    0.36754   3.042  0.00235 ** 
## loan_percent_income        11.02573    0.24897  44.285  < 2e-16 ***
## cb_person_default_on_file   0.06397    0.06330   1.011  0.31219    
## cb_person_cred_hist_length  0.21297    0.31925   0.667  0.50471    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 21053  on 20045  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 13524  on 20023  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 13570
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6
# Fazendo previsões no conjunto de teste NORMALIZADO
previsoes_prob <- predict(modelo_logistico, 
                          newdata = teste_norm, 
                          type = "response")

# Convertendo as probabilidades em classes (0 ou 1)
previsoes_classe <- ifelse(previsoes_prob > 0.3, 1, 0)

# Visualizando primeiras previsões
head(previsoes_classe)
##  1  6  8 12 24 25 
##  1  0  1  1  0  1
# Matriz de confusão
table(predicted = previsoes_classe, 
      actual = teste_norm$loan_status)
##          actual
## predicted    0    1
##         0 6023  509
##         1  748 1312
# Pacote caret
library(caret)

confusionMatrix(as.factor(previsoes_classe), 
                as.factor(teste_norm$loan_status),
                positive = "1")
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction    0    1
##          0 6023  509
##          1  748 1312
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.8537          
##                  95% CI : (0.8461, 0.8611)
##     No Information Rate : 0.7881          
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16       
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.5821          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 1.908e-11       
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.7205          
##             Specificity : 0.8895          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.6369          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.9221          
##              Prevalence : 0.2119          
##          Detection Rate : 0.1527          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.2398          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.8050          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : 1               
## 
#drop1(modelo_logistico, test = "Chisq")

# ---------------- ROC e AUC ----------------

library(pROC)

roc_curve <- roc(teste_norm$loan_status, previsoes_prob)

auc_value <- auc(roc_curve)
cat("AUC: ", auc_value, "\n")
## AUC:  0.8710323
plot(roc_curve, 
     main = "Curva ROC - Regressão Logística (Normalizado)", 
     col = "blue", 
     lwd = 2, 
     print.thres = TRUE)

# -------------------------------
# LASSO - Regressão Logística Penalizada
# -------------------------------

library(glmnet)

# Criando matriz de preditores (sem intercepto)
x_treino <- model.matrix(loan_status ~ ., treino_norm)[, -1]
y_treino <- as.numeric(treino_norm$loan_status) - 1  # converter para 0/1

# Ajustando LASSO com validação cruzada
modelo_lasso <- cv.glmnet(
  x_treino,
  y_treino,
  family = "binomial",
  alpha = 1   # alpha = 1 → LASSO
)

# Melhor lambda encontrado
best_lambda <- modelo_lasso$lambda.min
cat("Melhor lambda:", best_lambda, "\n")
## Melhor lambda: 0.0008594084
# -------------------------------
# Previsões no teste
# -------------------------------

x_teste <- model.matrix(loan_status ~ ., teste_norm)[, -1]

previsoes_prob_lasso <- predict(
  modelo_lasso,
  newx = x_teste,
  s = "lambda.min",
  type = "response"
)

# Converter probabilidades em classe
previsoes_classe_lasso <- ifelse(previsoes_prob_lasso > 0.5, 1, 0)

# -------------------------------
# Avaliação
# -------------------------------

#library(caret)

confusionMatrix(
  as.factor(previsoes_classe_lasso),
  as.factor(teste_norm$loan_status),
  positive = "1")
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction    0    1
##          0 6479  808
##          1  292 1013
##                                          
##                Accuracy : 0.872          
##                  95% CI : (0.8647, 0.879)
##     No Information Rate : 0.7881         
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16      
##                                          
##                   Kappa : 0.5725         
##                                          
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16      
##                                          
##             Sensitivity : 0.5563         
##             Specificity : 0.9569         
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.7762         
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.8891         
##              Prevalence : 0.2119         
##          Detection Rate : 0.1179         
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.1519         
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.7566         
##                                          
##        'Positive' Class : 1              
## 
# -------------------------------
# ROC e AUC
# -------------------------------

#library(pROC)

roc_lasso <- roc(teste_norm$loan_status, as.numeric(previsoes_prob_lasso))

auc_lasso <- auc(roc_lasso)
cat("AUC LASSO:", auc_lasso, "\n")
## AUC LASSO: 0.871274
plot(roc_lasso,
     main = "Curva ROC - LASSO",
     col = "red",
     lwd = 2,
     print.thres = TRUE)

#...............................................................................
# ---- Random Forest ----
# 1) Pacotes
library(randomForest)
library(caret)
library(pROC)

set.seed(123)  # Reprodutibilidade

############################################################
# 2) Garantir variável resposta correta
############################################################

treino_data$loan_status <- as.factor(treino_data$loan_status)
teste_data$loan_status  <- as.factor(teste_data$loan_status)

############################################################
# 3) Modelo Random Forest
############################################################

modelo_rf <- randomForest(
  loan_status ~ .,
  data = treino_data,
  importance = TRUE,
  ntree = 300,
  mtry = floor(sqrt(ncol(treino_data) - 1)), 
  nodesize = 10  # Árvores menos profundas (evita overfitting)
)
print(modelo_rf)
## 
## Call:
##  randomForest(formula = loan_status ~ ., data = treino_data, importance = TRUE,      ntree = 300, mtry = floor(sqrt(ncol(treino_data) - 1)), nodesize = 10) 
##                Type of random forest: classification
##                      Number of trees: 300
## No. of variables tried at each split: 3
## 
##         OOB estimate of  error rate: 6.75%
## Confusion matrix:
##       0    1 class.error
## 0 15582   82 0.005234934
## 1  1271 3111 0.290050205
############################################################
# 4) Importância das variáveis
############################################################

importance(modelo_rf)
##                                   0          1 MeanDecreaseAccuracy
## person_age                 27.30750   5.379153             28.32586
## person_income              60.44852  39.463074             68.38336
## person_home_ownership      94.31049 143.891482            137.92897
## person_emp_length          55.53304  62.428085             78.00631
## loan_intent                75.47349  92.996665             96.80787
## loan_grade                 66.38551  21.924259             86.51517
## loan_amnt                  35.30968   7.247133             38.47016
## loan_int_rate              28.55313  14.875730             28.09415
## loan_percent_income        67.04923  96.049079            101.26050
## cb_person_default_on_file  10.83775  -5.967359             10.97248
## cb_person_cred_hist_length 19.69338   4.197878             20.77466
##                            MeanDecreaseGini
## person_age                        153.94559
## person_income                     768.85530
## person_home_ownership             672.04276
## person_emp_length                 303.37961
## loan_intent                       476.14206
## loan_grade                        764.74341
## loan_amnt                         335.52564
## loan_int_rate                     617.12588
## loan_percent_income              1350.02872
## cb_person_default_on_file          51.30298
## cb_person_cred_hist_length        112.21896
varImpPlot(modelo_rf)

############################################################
# 5) Probabilidades (PD)
############################################################

probabilidades_rf <- predict(
  modelo_rf,
  newdata = teste_data,
  type = "prob"
)
pd_rf <- probabilidades_rf[, 2]  # Probabilidade de default

############################################################
# 6) Brier Score (Qualidade probabilística)
############################################################

brier_rf <- mean(
  (pd_rf -
     as.numeric(as.character(teste_data$loan_status)))^2
)
cat("Brier Score:", brier_rf, "\n")
## Brier Score: 0.05516165
############################################################
# 7) Curva ROC e AUC
############################################################

roc_curve <- roc(
  teste_data$loan_status,
  pd_rf
)

plot(roc_curve,
     main = "Curva ROC - Random Forest",
     col = "red",
     lwd = 2)

auc_value <- auc(roc_curve)
cat("AUC:", auc_value, "\n")
## AUC: 0.9311523
############################################################
# 8) Estatística KS (muito usada em crédito)
############################################################

dados_ks <- data.frame(
  PD = pd_rf,
  y  = as.numeric(as.character(teste_data$loan_status))
)

dados_ks <- dados_ks[order(dados_ks$PD), ]

cum_good <- cumsum(dados_ks$y == 0) / sum(dados_ks$y == 0)
cum_bad  <- cumsum(dados_ks$y == 1) / sum(dados_ks$y == 1)

ks_value <- max(abs(cum_good - cum_bad))

cat("KS:", ks_value, "\n")
## KS: 0.7377353
############################################################
# 9) Threshold ótimo (opcional, mais realista que 0.5)
############################################################

threshold_otimo <- coords(
  roc_curve,
  "best",
  ret = "threshold",
  transpose = FALSE
)

threshold_otimo <- as.numeric(threshold_otimo)

cat("Threshold ótimo:", threshold_otimo, "\n")
## Threshold ótimo: 0.215
############################################################
# 10) Comparação de Cutoffs
############################################################

# Cutoff padrão 0.5
classe_05 <- ifelse(pd_rf > 0.5, 1, 0)

cm_05 <- confusionMatrix(
  as.factor(classe_05),
  as.factor(teste_data$loan_status),
  positive = "1"
)

# Cutoff ótimo ROC
classe_otima <- ifelse(pd_rf > threshold_otimo, 1, 0)

cm_otima <- confusionMatrix(
  as.factor(classe_otima),
  as.factor(teste_data$loan_status),
  positive = "1"
)

cat("=== Cutoff 0.5 ===\n")
## === Cutoff 0.5 ===
print(cm_05)
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction    0    1
##          0 6743  522
##          1   28 1299
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.936           
##                  95% CI : (0.9306, 0.9411)
##     No Information Rate : 0.7881          
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16       
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.7873          
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : < 2.2e-16       
##                                           
##             Sensitivity : 0.7133          
##             Specificity : 0.9959          
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.9789          
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.9281          
##              Prevalence : 0.2119          
##          Detection Rate : 0.1512          
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.1544          
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.8546          
##                                           
##        'Positive' Class : 1               
## 
cat("\n=== Cutoff Ótimo ROC ===\n")
## 
## === Cutoff Ótimo ROC ===
print(cm_otima)
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction    0    1
##          0 6393  377
##          1  378 1444
##                                          
##                Accuracy : 0.9121         
##                  95% CI : (0.9059, 0.918)
##     No Information Rate : 0.7881         
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : <2e-16         
##                                          
##                   Kappa : 0.737          
##                                          
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 1              
##                                          
##             Sensitivity : 0.7930         
##             Specificity : 0.9442         
##          Pos Pred Value : 0.7925         
##          Neg Pred Value : 0.9443         
##              Prevalence : 0.2119         
##          Detection Rate : 0.1681         
##    Detection Prevalence : 0.2121         
##       Balanced Accuracy : 0.8686         
##                                          
##        'Positive' Class : 1              
## 
#...............................................................................

############################################################
# PREVER PERDAS, LIMITE E JUROS
############################################################

# 1) Probabilidade de Default (PD)
pd <- predict(modelo_rf, newdata = teste_data, type="prob")[,2]

teste_data$PD <- pd
teste_data$PD_base <- pd

############################################################
# MELHORIA 1: BRIER SCORE
############################################################

brier_rf <- mean(
  (teste_data$PD_base -
     as.numeric(as.character(teste_data$loan_status)))^2
)
cat("Brier Score:", brier_rf, "\n")
## Brier Score: 0.05516165
############################################################
#  MELHORIA 2: CALIBRAÇÃO (Platt Scaling)
############################################################

pd_train_rf <- predict(modelo_rf,
                       newdata = treino_data,
                       type="prob")[,2]

cal_model <- glm(treino_data$loan_status ~ pd_train_rf,
                 family = binomial)

pd_rf_cal <- predict(cal_model,
                     newdata = data.frame(pd_train_rf = pd),
                     type="response")

# agora substituímos PD_base pela calibrada
teste_data$PD_base <- pd_rf_cal
teste_data$PD_final <- pd_rf_cal

############################################################
# 2) Parâmetros econômicos
############################################################

LGD <- 0.6
taxa_base <- 0.25
alpha <- 2   # intensidade do aumento de risco com limite

teste_data$LGD <- LGD
teste_data$EAD <- teste_data$loan_amnt


# 3) Expected Loss e Lucro com limite histórico
# Agora usando PD calibrada

teste_data$EL <- teste_data$PD_final *
  LGD *
  teste_data$EAD

teste_data$receita <- teste_data$EAD * taxa_base

teste_data$lucro_esperado <-
  teste_data$receita - teste_data$EL

cat("Lucro total com política atual:",
    sum(teste_data$lucro_esperado), "\n")
## Lucro total com política atual: 8863944
############################################################
# OTIMIZAÇÃO DE LIMITE
############################################################

calcular_pd <- function(pd_base, L, L_ref, alpha){
  fator <- (L / L_ref)^alpha
  pd_novo <- pd_base * fator
  pd_novo <- pmin(pd_novo, 0.99)
  return(pd_novo)
}

calcular_lucro <- function(L, pd_base,
                           L_ref, taxa, LGD, alpha){
  pd_novo <- calcular_pd(pd_base,
                         L, L_ref, alpha)
  lucro <- L * (taxa - pd_novo * LGD)
  return(lucro)
}

teste_data$limite_otimo <- NA
teste_data$lucro_otimo <- NA

for(i in 1:nrow(teste_data)){
  
  pd_i <- teste_data$PD_final[i]   # usando PD calibrada
  L_ref <- teste_data$loan_amnt[i]
  
  limites_possiveis <- seq(1000, 30000, by=1000)
  
  lucros <- sapply(limites_possiveis, function(L){
    calcular_lucro(L,
                   pd_i,
                   L_ref,
                   taxa_base,
                   LGD,
                   alpha)
  })
  
  melhor_indice <- which.max(lucros)
  
  teste_data$limite_otimo[i] <-
    limites_possiveis[melhor_indice]
  
  teste_data$lucro_otimo[i] <-
    lucros[melhor_indice]
}

cat("Lucro total com limite otimizado:",
    sum(teste_data$lucro_otimo), "\n")
## Lucro total com limite otimizado: 37019335
############################################################
# POLÍTICA FINAL DE APROVAÇÃO
############################################################

teste_data$aprovar <-
  ifelse(teste_data$lucro_otimo > 0, 1, 0)

lucro_final <-
  sum(teste_data$lucro_otimo[
    teste_data$aprovar == 1
  ])

cat("Lucro final após rejeição:",
    lucro_final, "\n")
## Lucro final após rejeição: 37028362
cat("Clientes aprovados:",
    sum(teste_data$aprovar), "\n")
## Clientes aprovados: 8545
############################################################
# PRECIFICAÇÃO BASEADA EM RISCO
############################################################

teste_data$taxa_minima <-
  teste_data$PD_final * LGD   #  calibrada

teste_data$taxa_sugerida <-
  pmin(0.60,
       0.10 + 2 * teste_data$PD_final)

summary(teste_data$taxa_sugerida)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##  0.1034  0.1050  0.1112  0.2302  0.2693  0.6000
############################################################
# RESUMO FINAL
############################################################

cat("Limite médio concedido:",
    mean(teste_data$limite_otimo[
      teste_data$aprovar == 1
    ]), "\n")
## Limite médio concedido: 20700.64
cat("Lucro médio por cliente aprovado:",
    mean(teste_data$lucro_otimo[
      teste_data$aprovar == 1
    ]), "\n")
## Lucro médio por cliente aprovado: 4333.337
############################################################
### VALIDAÇÃO COMPLETA DO MODELO DE CRÉDITO
############################################################


############################################################
### 1) VALIDAÇÃO DO MODELO DE RISCO (PD)
############################################################

library(pROC)
library(dplyr)

dados_validacao <- teste_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(PD_final) &
           !is.na(loan_status))

roc_obj <- roc(dados_validacao$loan_status,
               dados_validacao$PD_final)

auc_valor <- auc(roc_obj)

cat("AUC do modelo:", auc_valor, "\n")
## AUC do modelo: 0.9311523
plot(roc_obj,
     main="Curva ROC",
     print.thres = TRUE)

############################################################
### 1.2 Estatística KS
############################################################

ks_table <- dados_validacao %>%
  arrange(PD_final) %>%
  mutate(
    cum_good = cumsum(loan_status == 0) /
      sum(loan_status == 0),
    cum_bad  = cumsum(loan_status == 1) /
      sum(loan_status == 1),
    ks = abs(cum_good - cum_bad)
  )

ks_valor <- max(ks_table$ks)

cat("KS do modelo:", ks_valor, "\n")
## KS do modelo: 0.7377353
############################################################
### 1.3 Calibração por Decis
############################################################

dados_validacao$loan_status <-
  as.numeric(as.character(
    dados_validacao$loan_status
  ))

calibracao <- dados_validacao %>%
  mutate(decile = ntile(PD_final, 10)) %>%
  group_by(decile) %>%
  summarise(
    PD_medio = mean(PD_final),
    Default_real = mean(loan_status),
    Qtde = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(calibracao)
## # A tibble: 10 × 4
##    decile PD_medio Default_real  Qtde
##     <int>    <dbl>        <dbl> <int>
##  1      1  0.00175      0.00465   860
##  2      2  0.00203      0.0198    860
##  3      3  0.00249      0.0268    859
##  4      4  0.00325      0.0466    859
##  5      5  0.00467      0.0489    859
##  6      6  0.00779      0.0733    859
##  7      7  0.0190       0.0908    859
##  8      8  0.114        0.164     859
##  9      9  0.869        0.645     859
## 10     10  1.000        1         859
############################################################
### 2) VALIDAÇÃO ECONÔMICA DA POLÍTICA
############################################################

lucro_atual <- sum(teste_data$lucro_esperado,
                   na.rm=TRUE)

lucro_otimizado <- sum(teste_data$lucro_otimo,
                       na.rm=TRUE)

lucro_aprovando <- sum(
  teste_data$lucro_otimo[
    teste_data$lucro_otimo > 0
  ],
  na.rm=TRUE
)

comparacao <- data.frame(
  Politica = c("Atual",
               "Limite Otimizado",
               "Otimizado + Rejeicao"),
  Lucro = c(lucro_atual,
            lucro_otimizado,
            lucro_aprovando)
)

print(comparacao)
##               Politica    Lucro
## 1                Atual  8863944
## 2     Limite Otimizado 37019335
## 3 Otimizado + Rejeicao 37028362
############################################################
### 2.2 Métricas de Negócio
############################################################

EAD_total <- sum(teste_data$EAD,
                 na.rm=TRUE)

EL_total  <- sum(teste_data$EL,
                 na.rm=TRUE)

ROA_esperado <- lucro_aprovando / EAD_total
loss_rate <- EL_total / EAD_total
approval_rate <- mean(teste_data$aprovar,
                      na.rm=TRUE)

cat("ROA esperado:", ROA_esperado, "\n")
## ROA esperado: 0.4482247
cat("Loss Rate:", loss_rate, "\n")
## Loss Rate: 0.1427028
cat("Approval Rate:", approval_rate, "\n")
## Approval Rate: 0.9945298
